locked_facts: Motional EMF: ε = BLv (conductor of length L moving at velocity v perpendicular to B)
Cause: Lorentz force on free electrons in the moving conductor: F = qv × B
Electrons accumulate at one end → potential difference (EMF) builds up
Equilibrium: electric force = magnetic force → qE = qvB → E = vB → ε = EL = BLv
For a rotating rod: ε = ½BωL² (each element at different distance from pivot)
Motional EMF is consistent with Faraday's law: ε = -dΦ/dt = BLv (area sweeping rate)
truth_statements: When a conductor moves through a magnetic field the free charges experience a Lorentz force that creates an EMF
The motional EMF equals BLv when the conductor moves perpendicular to both its length and the field
The motional EMF can be derived either from the Lorentz force on charges or from Faraday's law — both give the same result
A rod rotating in a magnetic field generates EMF ε = ½BωL² between its centre and tip
The mechanical work done in moving the conductor against the magnetic braking force equals the electrical energy generated

aha_moment.what_to_show: A rod sliding on rails in a magnetic field. Free electrons inside the rod experience Lorentz force F = qv×B pushing them to one end. Charge accumulates until electric repulsion balances magnetic force. The resulting EMF drives current through the circuit — mechanical energy converts to electrical.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Electrons moving to one end under Lorentz force, charge buildup, and current flowing in the external circuit
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see EMF as an abstract formula — seeing the Lorentz force physically separate charges makes motional EMF concrete

simulation_states.label: Rod in magnetic field
simulation_states.description: A conducting rod on parallel rails in uniform B field (into page). Rod is stationary — no force on electrons, no EMF.
simulation_states.emphasis: Stationary conductor in B = no EMF. Motion is needed.

simulation_states.label: Rod moves → electrons feel force
simulation_states.description: Rod moves right at velocity v. Free electrons experience F = qv×B (upward in rod). Electrons accumulate at top. EMF = BLv builds up. Current flows in circuit.
simulation_states.emphasis: Lorentz force separates charges → EMF develops → current flows

simulation_states.label: Faraday's law connection
simulation_states.description: The rod sweeps area ΔA = Lvdt in time dt. Flux change: ΔΦ = BΔA = BLvdt. ε = dΦ/dt = BLv. Same result from both approaches!
simulation_states.emphasis: Lorentz force approach and Faraday's law give IDENTICAL results — two views of the same physics

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts v, B, and L. EMF and current update. Power balance shown: mechanical power in = electrical power out. Rotating rod demo: ε = ½BωL².
simulation_states.emphasis: Increase speed → more EMF. The generator principle in its simplest form.

common_misconceptions.belief: Motional EMF requires a complete circuit
common_misconceptions.correction: EMF develops across the rod even without a circuit — the Lorentz force separates charges. A circuit is needed for CURRENT to flow, but the EMF (potential difference) exists regardless.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show charge separation in an isolated rod — voltmeter reads BLv even without a circuit

common_misconceptions.belief: The conductor must move perpendicular to B for any EMF
common_misconceptions.correction: EMF = BLv sinθ where θ is the angle between v and B. Only the perpendicular component of velocity contributes. At θ = 0 (parallel to B), EMF is zero.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show rod moving at different angles — EMF varies as sinθ
